features.title: pi-context clone — import a substrate from another project as a content-addressed fork
features.description: A dual-surface command (library cloneSubstrate + Pi tool + CLI + orchestrator script) that copies a source substrate's content (config vocabulary + all blocks + relations.json + objects/) into a destination dir, mints a fresh substrate_id, records a substrate_derived_from_substrate edge to the source, and writes a provenance-bearing registry entry. Fork semantics preserve the one-id<->one-dir invariant. Per DEC-0002.

features.title: pi-context git merge driver (+ post-merge integrity pass) — structure-aware 3-way merge of substrate files under git
features.description: A custom git merge driver (registered via .gitattributes + a [merge "pi-context"] driver entry) that performs structure-aware per-file 3-way merge of substrate files (<block>.json, relations.json, config, schemas) from git's %O/%A/%B — items by stable id/oid, edges as a set, ordered ref-fields via the convergent sequence field-type, config/schema — writing a data-model-valid result; plus a post-merge hook / merge-finalize command for cross-block referential integrity + invariant/lens revalidation. Git keeps branches, merge-base, refs, cherry-pick. Per DEC-0004.

features.title: Convergent ordered-sequence field-kind — CRDT-backed ordered item-refs that merge deterministically under concurrent insert/delete/move
features.description: A declared sequence-field kind over item-refs: a non-interleaving Fugue-class sequence CRDT (dense, immutable per-element position identifiers) plus a Kleppmann move-CRDT position register resolved by hybrid logical clock, with a sort-on-read projection to a plain ordered ref-array and array-position hand-edits translated to CRDT ops. CRDT state lives in the owning <block>.json; projection + field-aware merge live in block-api. One field-kind plugin into the substrate merge driver (FEAT-002). Per DEC-0005.

features.title: Substrate-derived state — config-driven context-current-state / context-status
features.description:
Today these ops hardwire their derivation to the stock kinds — focus / in-flight / ranked-next are computed from `framework-gaps` + `tasks`. On a custom-vocabulary substrate (e.g. `.workshopping` with `prompt-fragment` + `prompt-spec`) they return empty/inert: the substrate has a real "where are we / what's next," but the op can't see it because it's looking for kinds that aren't there.

Proposal: derive the derivation from active substrate's `config.json`, not from hardcoded kind names. The substrate declares its state-bearing vocabulary — which kind+predicate yields *focus*, which field gives *order/ranking*, which field+values mark *open/blocked/done*. Stock substrates declare `framework-gaps`+`tasks` (today's behavior preserved exactly); a custom substrate declares its own (e.g. `prompt-spec` ranked by `order`, blocked by unmet `deps`). With no declaration the op is a truthful "not configured," not a silent empty.

"where are we / what's next" becomes a capability *any* substrate opts into by declaring its own state fields, instead of a privilege of the stock project-management vocabulary.
features.motivation: context-current-state is fully inert on a custom-vocabulary substrate (confirmed: returns empty focus/inFlight/nextActions/blocked against .context-jit-spec-v2 whose state-bearing kinds are axioms/concepts/dispatch-modes, not tasks/framework-gaps), because currentState() hardcodes the stock kinds + the task_depends_on_task blocked-by relation + a P0-P3 rank. The where-are-we/what-next capability is thus a privilege of the stock PM vocabulary rather than something any substrate can opt into.

features.title: pi-context pi-bound: launch a pi session bound to the composed extension tool surface
features.description: A globally-runnable `pi-context pi-bound` command launches an interactive pi session restricted to the composed extension tool surface — the static SKILL.md tool union plus the config-declared per-target composites — reproducing scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh as a first-class CLI command and subsuming it.
features.motivation: The constrained-launch capability exists only as an out-of-tree shell script that needs the repo checkout. As a command on a globally installed pi-context, it runs from any project with the published packages present.

features.title: pi-context update — a unified, drift-aware, customization-preserving update command with --dry-run
features.description: A single `pi-context update` command brings an installed substrate's model (schemas + catalog-origin blocks) to a newer pi-context release while preserving the user's customizations: it consults the checkStatus drift classification, preserves or merges locally-modified schemas instead of overwriting (FGAP-046), surfaces every mutation it makes, and with --dry-run previews the full change set without writing. It supersedes the bare `/context install --update` flag as the user-facing update path, and states the guarantee that config registries, populated blocks, and user-added elements are never clobbered.
features.motivation: The framework exists to be customized; an update must not punish customization. Today update is a flag on install that overwrites locally-modified schemas, side-writes migration declarations unsurfaced, and never consults the drift detector that already exists. A unified command with a dry-run preview makes update safe and legible.

features.title: Convention-articulation enforcement — the validator requires planning artifacts to cite a governing convention or a missing-convention gap
features.description: context-validate requires every decision, feature, and task to articulate its governance: an item_governed_by_convention edge to a convention, OR an item_acknowledges_missing_convention edge to a 'no convention governs X' framework-gap. A planning artifact filed with neither is a validation violation, so conventions cannot be silently skipped and a missing convention surfaces as tracked debt rather than a silent opt-out.
features.motivation: Conventions govern planning before implementation, but their composition into context relies on the LLM reading and heeding them. Enforcing articulation at validation makes governance a checked precondition, not an aspiration.

features.title: pi-context-cli best-of-breed surface — script-parity input/output, discoverability, and write-safety to a superior CLI UX
features.description: The reflecting pi-context CLI is both the dogfooding surface and the new-user entry point; today it lags its op-twin orchestrator scripts on input ergonomics, human output, discoverability, error UX, and write-safety. This feature brings the CLI to best-of-breed parity additively. INVARIANT (every task preserves it): the reflecting CLI is additive — every currently-working invocation keeps working, and every in-pi op's behavior plus the --json data envelope are byte-unchanged; new affordances (kebab/alias flags, input shorthands, render modes, previews, granular exit codes, help/version) are layers over the canonical op surface, never replacements. The sole authorized in-pi op change is the read-schema addressed-node correction (FGAP-027), paired with its CLI-side twin so read-schema is coherent across both surfaces. A behavioral parity gate locks the input/output parity classes at commit so the regression class is caught-at-commit, not found-by-dogfooding.
features.motivation: The CLI is the surface the project is built and demonstrated on; every parity/discoverability/safety gap degrades both dogfooding and first-run UX. The standard is best-of-breed with a superior user experience, sequenced by priority, nothing tiered as ship-after.

features.title: update blocked-diagnostic + resolution loop — story-driven
features.description: Bring pi-context update's blocked outcome from an opaque dead-end to a diagnosed, resolvable state, and make the dry-run predict the live outcome. The work container for the update-arc gap set: FGAP-066 (dry/live outcome parity), FGAP-077 (per-item validation diagnostic), FGAP-030+FGAP-078 (check-status exposed as a CLI op + version-gap reporting), FGAP-079 (catalog schema locally diffable), FGAP-080 (pending-blocked state + resolve-blocked / update --continue), FGAP-081 (in-file failure markers, object-store recoverable). INVARIANT (every task preserves it): the per-schema byte-exact rollback and per-component application (DEC-0018) are preserved; no data loss on any blocked path; the only blocked-path write is the explicitly-declared marker-mode of FGAP-081, with the pre-marker block recoverable from the object store; in-pi op behavior changes only where a gap names it. The feature's success criteria are the user stories it advances — STORY-001..014, filed verbatim from the operator's in-pi reports — not embedded criteria text.
features.motivation: An operator hit blocked on a live update the dry-run had predicted safe, with no diagnostic naming the failing item/field and no resolution path — the inciting incident the 14 user stories articulate. The standard is best-of-breed: diagnose, fix, retry, with the dry-run telling the truth.

features.title: Substrate evolution safety + currency — catalog/config/schema evolution and substrate read/repair/materialize never brick or silently corrupt an installed substrate
features.description: Catalog, config, and schema evolution, and every read / repair / materialize of an already-installed substrate, preserve its loadability, the integrity of its install/validity signals, and its recoverability through sanctioned ops. Config carries a versioned envelope and migrates on load across a version gap; an invalid config is recoverable through a sanctioned repair that validates the result, not the pre-existing state; a non-additive config-schema diff is flagged at build time unless paired with a migration; block data carries schema_version so read-time validation and migration are active; a block kind declared after install is materialized into the existing substrate. Invariant: no evolution, read, repair, or materialize leaves the substrate bricked, silently corrupted, or unrecoverable through sanctioned ops.
